text: This case shows an abdominal perineal excision of rectum with vertical rectus myocutaneous flap reconstruction of the perineum. The patient is sixty-seven years old and presented to a neighboring unit with a locally advanced lower third rectal cancer and a fistula in anus which was assumed to be malignant. She had received preoperative chemoradiotherapy ten weeks before surgery. This patient has undergone a previous open appendicectomy. For this reason, a left VRAM flap was chosen.

The plastic surgery team marks the midline around the umbilicus for the intended flap. An extended VRAM flap is marked on the left side in a sigmoid shape, measuring 30 by 8 centimeters. A midline incision is made from the cranial marking to the mons pubis and down to the linea alba. Dissection proceeds laterally in a suprafacial plane until perforating vessels are visible through the fascia of the anterior rectus sheath. The anterior sheath is incised lateral to the rectus muscle's medial edge, preserving perforating vessels on the flap. The rectus muscle is reflected laterally, exposing the posterior rectus sheath, and its medial edge is secured to the overlying fat. A prominent umbilical branch from the deep inferior epigastric artery is noted medially on the rectus muscle's undersurface at the arcuate line level.

The surgeon opens the center of the posterior rectus sheath to enter the abdomen in a paramedian position. Inside the abdominal cavity, the left and sigmoid colon are mobilized, and the left ureter is identified and protected. The origin of the inferior mesenteric artery is located, and a high division is performed.

After dividing the inferior mesenteric artery, the surgeon uses a linear stapler to divide the colon. The rectal dissection is initiated, mobilizing the rectum circumferentially in the mesorectal plane to a level above the pelvic floor. A wide incision is made in the perianal skin to address the presumed malignant fistula, and dissection proceeds through the ischio-rectal fossa bilaterally. Posteriorly, the surgeon develops a plane anterior to the tip of the coccyx.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to divide the pelvic floor circumferentially near its insertion into the bony pelvis. The perineal dissection is completed by dividing the posterior vaginal wall in conjunction with the specimen, utilizing the ligature device to minimize bleeding.

The surgical specimen shows minimal wasting at the pelvic floor level, characteristic of a cylindrical abdominoperineal excision of the rectum. After tumor removal, the abdomen is temporarily closed with silk sutures and skin staples. The lateral and superior margins of the VRAM flap are incised down to the deep fascia, and the rectus muscle is divided at the costal margin. The superior epigastric artery is divided and ligated. The flap is dissected cranially to caudally, dividing segmental neurovascular pedicles laterally. The deep inferior epigastric artery pedicle is identified and skeletonized to prevent kinking during flap transposition. The flap is rotated 180 degrees on its long axis, with the cranial tip passed through the pelvis into the perineum, marking the skin island extent. The flap is then returned through the abdomen, and excess skin is removed.

The surgeon positions the flap through the pelvis without tension on the pedicle. From the perineal approach, the proximal edge of the skin island on the flap is sutured to the vaginal remnant, replacing its posterior wall. Suturing proceeds along the cut edge of the vagina, proximal to the cervix, and down either side until reaching the perineal skin. The flap is temporarily secured within the skin defect with staples, followed by closure with dermal sutures and nylon in the skin. The abdomen is closed in layers, including the posterior and anterior sheaths, and a colostomy is formed.
